manifest.name: Learning Science & Socrates
manifest.description: A complete demo library covering spaced repetition, FSRS, concept maps, event sourcing, and mastery certification — designed to exercise every Socrates content type.

content.title: Active Recall
content.definition: The learning strategy of retrieving information from memory rather than passively re-reading.
content.summary: Active recall strengthens memory traces through retrieval practice. It is the foundation of effective spaced repetition systems.

content.title: The Forgetting Curve
content.definition: Ebbinghaus's model showing memory retention declines exponentially over time without reinforcement.
content.summary: Understanding the forgetting curve motivates spaced repetition: reviews must occur before retention drops below useful thresholds.

content.title: Spaced Repetition
content.definition: A scheduling method that increases intervals between reviews as material becomes better learned.
content.summary: Spaced repetition combats the forgetting curve by timing reviews at optimal intervals. Modern systems use algorithms like FSRS to personalize schedules.

content.title: FSRS (Free Spaced Repetition Scheduler)
content.definition: A modern spaced repetition algorithm that models memory stability and difficulty to predict optimal review intervals.
content.summary: FSRS uses stability (how long memory lasts) and difficulty (how hard the item is) to schedule reviews. Socrates uses FSRS v6 for card scheduling.

content.title: Concept Maps
content.definition: A structured representation of knowledge as concepts connected by typed relationships and prerequisites.
content.summary: Socrates treats concept maps as first-class: scheduling, certification, and relationship cards all operate over the concept graph.

content.title: Event Sourcing in Socrates
content.definition: An architecture where all learning actions are recorded as immutable events, and read models (views) are derived by projectors.
content.summary: When you review a card, Socrates appends a card_reviewed event. Cloud Functions project that into schedule and performance views.

content.title: Mastery Certification
content.definition: A structured assessment flow that certifies concept-level mastery and may suppress or accelerate related cards.
content.summary: Certification uses targeted questions across a concept's card set. Outcomes (full, partial, none) affect scheduling policy.

editorial.notes: Retrieval practice context helps interpret why retention decays between reviews.
Concept maps organize the knowledge that retrieval practice strengthens.
You must understand retrieval practice before interval scheduling makes sense.
The forgetting curve motivates why spacing matters.
FSRS is a specific algorithmic implementation of spaced repetition.
Concept maps organize what spaced repetition schedules.
Certification outcomes affect FSRS scheduling (suppression, acceleration).

content.front: What is active recall?
content.back: Retrieving information from memory (testing yourself) rather than passively re-reading.

content.front: What is the testing effect?
content.back: The finding that retrieval practice improves long-term retention more than restudying the same material.

content.front: Why is highlighting alone a weak study strategy?
content.back: It is passive — it does not require retrieval. Without recall, the testing effect does not apply.

content.front: Who discovered the forgetting curve, and what does it show?
content.back: Hermann Ebbinghaus — memory retention decays exponentially over time without reinforcement.

content.front: Fill in the blanks.
content.back: Without review, retention drops sharply within the first [[cloze_1]].

cloze_data.template_text: Without review, retention drops sharply within the first [[cloze_1]].

content.front: What is spaced repetition?
content.back: Increasing review intervals as material is learned, combating the forgetting curve with minimal total study time.

content.front: Spaced repetition vs. cramming: which produces better long-term retention?
content.back: Spaced repetition — cramming may boost short-term performance but fails to build durable memory.

content.front: How does spaced repetition relate to active recall?
content.back: Each spaced review is an active recall event — spacing determines when, recall is what you do.

content.front: In FSRS, what is stability?
content.back: The predicted interval (in days) at which retrievability drops to 90% — how long the memory lasts.

content.front: In FSRS, what is difficulty?
content.back: An intrinsic property of the card (1–10) representing how hard it is to remember — higher difficulty means shorter intervals.

content.front: What are the four FSRS review grades?
content.back: Again, Hard, Good, Easy — each updates stability and difficulty differently.

content.front: Complete the sentence.
content.back: After a 'Good' review, FSRS increases [[cloze_1]] and may slightly adjust [[cloze_2]].

cloze_data.template_text: After a 'Good' review, FSRS increases [[cloze_1]] and may slightly adjust [[cloze_2]].

content.front: What is a concept map in Socrates?
content.back: A graph of concepts connected by typed relationships (prerequisites, mechanisms, etc.) with linked cards and questions.

content.front: Why do prerequisites matter in a concept map?
content.back: They enforce learning order — you should master foundational concepts before dependent ones.

content.front: What is event sourcing?
content.back: Storing state changes as an immutable log of events; current state is derived by processing those events.

content.front: What is a projector in Socrates?
content.back: A server function that reads events and updates read-model views (e.g. card_reviewed → card schedule view).

content.front: Why must event writes be idempotent?
content.back: Offline sync may retry uploads — duplicate events must not double-apply schedule changes.

content.front: What are the three mastery certification outcomes?
content.back: Full, partial, or none — based on performance across certification questions for a concept.

content.front: What events does mastery certification produce?
content.back: mastery_certification_started and mastery_certification_completed — the latter updates ConceptCertificationView.

content.front: Why must you understand active recall before spaced repetition?
content.back: Because spaced repetition schedules retrieval events — without understanding recall, spacing is just arbitrary timing.

content.front: How does the forgetting curve motivate spaced repetition?
content.back: It shows memory decays over time — spacing reviews before retention drops minimizes total study while preserving knowledge.

content.stem: Which study activity best exemplifies active recall?

options.text: Re-reading highlighted notes
Closing the book and writing everything you remember
Watching a lecture video again
Copying definitions verbatim

explanations.general: Active recall requires retrieval from memory without cues.

options.text: More study time always equals better retention
Retrieval practice improves long-term retention vs. restudy
Cramming is optimal for durable memory
Passive review outperforms quizzing

explanations.general: Retrieval strengthens memory more than passive restudy of the same material.

content.stem: According to the forgetting curve, memory retention without review:

options.text: Stays flat indefinitely
Decays exponentially over time
Improves with time
Depends only on initial study duration

explanations.general: Ebbinghaus showed rapid initial decay, then slower decline.

content.stem: A student reviews all cards every day regardless of performance. What misconception does this reflect?

options.text: Spacing intervals should adapt as material is learned
Fixed daily review is always optimal
Active recall is unnecessary with enough repetition
The forgetting curve does not apply to flashcards

explanations.general: Spaced repetition increases intervals — fixed daily review ignores learned stability.

distractors.opt_A: This is the correct principle, not a misconception.

content.stem: In FSRS, a learner marks every card 'Again' even when they knew the answer. What is the likely scheduling impact?

options.text: Stability increases rapidly
Intervals stay short; stability does not grow
Difficulty drops to minimum
Cards are permanently suppressed

explanations.general: 'Again' signals failed recall — FSRS will not extend intervals.

content.stem: A card with high FSRS difficulty will tend to have:

options.text: Longer intervals than an easy card
Shorter intervals than an easy card
No scheduled reviews
Identical intervals regardless of grade

explanations.general: Higher difficulty means the item is harder to retain — FSRS schedules more frequent reviews.

content.stem: In a Socrates concept map, a 'prerequisite' relationship means:

options.text: Both concepts must be studied simultaneously
The from-concept should be learned before the to-concept
The concepts are unrelated
Only certification questions link the concepts

explanations.general: Prerequisites enforce learning order in the knowledge graph.

content.stem: A developer updates the card schedule document directly in Firestore instead of appending events. What breaks?

options.text: Nothing — views are the source of truth
Audit trail and idempotent replay from the event log
Only the UI theme
Concept map layout

explanations.general: Event sourcing derives state from events — direct view edits bypass the log.

options.text: Suppress all cards in the library permanently
Apply policy based on outcome — may adjust scheduling for related cards
Delete the concept from the map
Skip FSRS entirely

explanations.general: Certification outcomes (full/partial/none) drive scheduling policy, not blanket suppression.

distractors.opt_A: Partial mastery does not mean permanent suppression of everything.

content.stem: Why is active recall a prerequisite for understanding spaced repetition?

options.text: Spacing only works for visual learners
Spaced reviews are retrieval events — recall is what you do at each review
FSRS requires multiple-choice questions only
Active recall replaces the need for spacing

explanations.general: Spacing schedules when; active recall is the practice performed at each interval.
